title: Framewright in brief
text: Framewright is a rate-control engine that sits on top of standard video encoders, deciding how many bits to spend on each frame, and where within each frame, to hit a target quality or target bitrate as efficiently as possible. Rather than treating every frame or every region of a frame as equally important, Framewright allocates bits according to a perceptual quality model, spending more of the available bit budget on complex, high-motion, or visually salient content and less on flat, static, or less-noticeable regions. The engine supports two primary operating modes: a constant-rate-factor mode that targets a fixed perceptual quality level and lets output file size vary with content complexity, and a variable-bitrate mode that targets a fixed output bitrate and lets perceptual quality vary instead. Streaming platforms overwhelmingly use variable-bitrate mode to satisfy a strict bitrate ladder, while archival and video-on-demand libraries more often use constant-rate-factor mode to maximize quality within a storage or bandwidth budget that tolerates some variation.

title: GOP structure and reference frame defaults
text: Framewright's default group-of-pictures structure targets a 2-second interval, which at 30 frames per second works out to 60 frames between mandatory keyframes, with an adaptive scene-cut detector permitted to insert an additional keyframe early whenever a hard scene change would otherwise force an expensive intra-coded frame mid-GOP. The default B-frame pyramid depth is 4, meaning up to four levels of bidirectionally predicted frames reference one another hierarchically before ultimately resolving against a forward-predicted anchor frame. Maximum reference frame count defaults to 4, balancing improved compression efficiency from a larger reference window against the additional decoder memory and motion-search cost a larger window requires. These defaults are tuned for typical streaming content and are commonly overridden for specific use cases, such as low-latency live encoding, where B-frame depth is often reduced to cut encode-to-display latency.

title: Perceptual quality score targets by tier
text: Framewright scores every encoded output against an internal perceptual quality metric on a 0 to 100 scale, modeled after established perceptual video quality assessment methodology and calibrated against large-scale human subjective rating panels. The premium streaming tier targets a minimum perceptual score of 93 across 95% of encoded segments, while the standard tier targets a minimum score of 82, reflecting a deliberately lower bitrate budget for that tier. Segments falling below their tier's target score trigger an automatic bitrate-boost re-encode pass, which resolved 6.1% of standard-tier segments and 11.4% of premium-tier segments during the most recent reporting month, the premium tier's higher rate reflecting its tighter quality floor rather than any difference in source content difficulty between tiers.

title: Encoding speed by resolution and preset
text:
Encoding throughput, measured as a multiple of real-time playback speed, varies substantially across resolution and the quality-versus-speed preset selected for a given job, since the highest-quality preset performs a far more exhaustive rate-distortion search per block.

| Resolution | Preset: fast | Preset: balanced | Preset: highest quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 720p | 14.9x | 6.8x | 2.2x |
| 1080p | 8.4x | 3.6x | 1.1x |
| 4K | 2.0x | 0.9x | 0.35x |

Video-on-demand libraries default to the highest-quality preset since encode time is rarely on the critical path, while live-streaming pipelines default to the balanced preset to keep encode-to-delivery latency within acceptable bounds under sustained load, reserving the fast preset mainly for preview renders and rapid iteration during pipeline testing.

title: Lagrangian rate-distortion optimization
text: Framewright's per-block mode and quantization decisions are driven by a Lagrangian rate-distortion cost function, J = D + lambda * R, where D is a perceptual distortion estimate for a given coding choice, R is the estimated bit cost of that choice, and lambda is a Lagrange multiplier that trades off quality against bitrate. A lower lambda favors spending more bits to reduce distortion, appropriate for constant-rate-factor mode where quality is the fixed target, while a higher lambda favors conserving bits, appropriate for variable-bitrate mode under a strict output budget. Framewright derives lambda from the target quantization level using an empirically fit relationship, lambda = a * QP^b, rather than a fixed theoretical formula, since the constants a and b are recalibrated periodically against the perceptual model's own ongoing tuning to keep the two systems consistent with one another.

title: Two-pass VOD encoding pipeline checklist
text:
Encoding a video-on-demand asset through Framewright's standard two-pass pipeline follows a fixed sequence to ensure the second pass can make informed bit-allocation decisions.
1. Run a fast first pass collecting per-frame complexity statistics without producing a final encoded bitstream.
2. Feed first-pass statistics into the per-title bitrate-ladder generator to select target bitrates for each rendition.
3. Run the full-quality second pass for each rendition, using first-pass statistics to guide bit allocation across the asset.
4. Score every output segment against the perceptual quality metric and flag any segment falling below its tier's target.
5. Trigger a bitrate-boost re-encode for any flagged segment before the asset is published.
Assets shorter than roughly 90 seconds skip the two-pass pipeline entirely and use single-pass constant-rate-factor encoding instead, since the fixed overhead of a first pass is not justified at that length.

title: Per-title encoding bitrate savings
text: Framewright's per-title encoding feature analyzes each asset's own complexity characteristics to generate a custom bitrate ladder rather than applying one fixed ladder uniformly across an entire content library. Measured across a sample of 40,000 recently encoded assets, per-title encoding reduced average bitrate by 19% at equivalent perceptual quality score compared to the fixed ladder it replaced. The savings are highly content-dependent: low-complexity animated content saw bitrate reductions as high as 34%, while high-motion sports and action content saw reductions closer to 8%, since such content already required close to the fixed ladder's provisioned bitrate to hit target quality. Aggregate storage savings from per-title encoding across the full content library are estimated at 22 petabytes as of the most recent quarterly report.

title: Errata: B-frame pyramid depth default
text: Errata notice filed against the version 5.6 configuration reference. An early printing of that reference stated: The default B-frame pyramid depth is 6, meaning up to four levels of bidirectionally predicted frames reference one another hierarchically before ultimately resolving against a forward-predicted anchor frame. That figure, 6, was carried over from an internal experimental configuration that was evaluated but never shipped as the default, and the sentence's own four-levels gloss makes plain it was a misprint. Readers relying on the value of 6 for capacity or latency planning should discard it. The correct default, 4, has applied to every shipped Framewright release since perceptual-quality-driven rate-distortion optimization was introduced. This errata has been folded into the current documentation build.

title: Vietnamese-language video-compression research
text: Framewright's per-title bitrate-ladder generator draws on Vietnamese video-compression research into content-adaptive encoding for streaming delivery. Nguyen and Tran's 2019 study, Tối ưu hóa thang đo bit theo nội dung cho truyền video trực tuyến thích ứng, on content-adaptive bitrate-ladder optimization for adaptive streaming, is cited in Framewright's ladder-generation design documentation for its analysis of how complexity-driven ladders should vary savings by genre rather than applying a uniform reduction target across all content. A related paper on mô hình đánh giá chất lượng tri giác cho video đa phân giải, perceptual quality assessment models for multi-resolution video, informed calibration of the 0 to 100 perceptual score against human subjective ratings. Framewright's engineering documentation credits both works in its per-title encoding rationale.

title: Choosing between CRF and VBR modes
text: An encoding job with no fixed output-size or bandwidth constraint, such as a VOD library prioritizing consistent perceived quality across a diverse content catalog, is generally best served by constant-rate-factor mode, since it targets a fixed perceptual outcome and lets output size follow content complexity naturally. A live-streaming job constrained by a fixed bitrate ladder that downstream players and CDN capacity planning both depend on must use variable-bitrate mode instead, since exceeding a rung's provisioned bitrate risks rebuffering for viewers on constrained connections regardless of how much the extra bits might have improved perceptual quality. Mixing modes within a single ladder, constant-rate-factor for some renditions and variable-bitrate for others, is technically possible but rare in practice, since it complicates downstream bitrate-adaptation logic that generally assumes every rung in a ladder behaves similarly under network pressure.

title: Platform scale statistics
text: As of the most recent operations report, Framewright encodes approximately 340,000 hours of source video per day across all partner streaming platforms combined, producing an average of 6.2 encoded renditions per hour of source content across a typical bitrate ladder. The encoded asset library under active management totals approximately 38 petabytes, following the storage reduction achieved through per-title encoding described elsewhere in this specification. Peak concurrent encoding throughput during a major live sporting event reached 4,100 simultaneous encoding jobs, the highest sustained load the platform has handled to date. Median time from source ingestion to a fully encoded, quality-verified VOD asset being ready for publication is 14 minutes for a typical 45-minute episode using the two-pass pipeline.

title: Perceptual score compliance by content genre
text:
Compliance with the premium tier's minimum perceptual score target of 93 varies meaningfully by content genre, reflecting how differently genres stress the rate-distortion optimizer under a fixed bit budget.

| Genre | Segments meeting target | Average re-encode rate |
|---|---|---|
| Animation | 99.1% | 1.2% |
| Drama / dialogue-heavy | 97.8% | 3.0% |
| Sports / high-motion | 88.6% | 11.4% |

Sports content's comparatively low compliance rate is the primary reason the perceptual model calibration effort has focused most recently on high-motion scene handling rather than on further gains in the already well-served animation and dialogue-heavy categories.

title: Finnish-language perceptual modeling research
text: Calibration of Framewright's perceptual quality model against human subjective ratings draws on Finnish research into motion-sensitive video quality assessment. Virtanen's 2020 study, Liikeherkkä videolaadun arviointimalli suurella kuvataajuudella, a motion-sensitive video quality assessment model for high frame rates, is cited in Framewright's model documentation for its finding that human perceptual sensitivity to compression artifacts changes substantially during fast camera or subject motion compared to static scenes. A related paper on urheilulähetysten pakkauksen optimointi suuren liikkeen kohtauksissa, compression optimization for sports broadcasts in high-motion scenes, directly informed later revisions to the perceptual model's handling of high-motion content. Framewright's engineering team credits this research specifically for identifying motion sensitivity as a distinct calibration axis from static-scene quality.

title: Live-streaming lookahead encoding procedure
text:
Live encoding jobs cannot use the standard two-pass pipeline, since a live stream has no complete asset to analyze in advance, so Framewright instead uses a bounded lookahead window to approximate some of two-pass encoding's benefit.
1. Buffer up to 150 frames, roughly 5 seconds at 30 frames per second, of incoming source video before beginning final encode of the oldest buffered frame.
2. Run lightweight complexity analysis across the buffered window to inform near-term bit allocation decisions.
3. Encode the oldest buffered frame using both its own content and the lookahead window's complexity statistics.
4. Slide the window forward by one frame and repeat continuously for the duration of the stream.
Streams requiring sub-second glass-to-glass latency typically reduce the lookahead window well below the 150-frame default, trading some rate-control accuracy for reduced end-to-end delay.

title: Glossary: lookahead window
text: Lookahead window (appendix definition). The buffer of not-yet-finally-encoded future frames a live encoding job holds in memory to inform near-term bit-allocation decisions before committing to a final encode of the oldest buffered frame. A larger lookahead window generally improves rate-control accuracy, since the encoder can anticipate an approaching scene change or motion burst before it arrives, at the direct cost of added glass-to-glass latency equal to the window's duration. Streams requiring sub-second glass-to-glass latency typically reduce the lookahead window well below the 220-frame default, trading some rate-control accuracy for reduced end-to-end delay. See also: two-pass encoding, glass-to-glass latency, scene-cut detection. Related glossary entries cover how the scene-cut detector itself operates within a bounded lookahead window rather than requiring full-asset analysis.

title: Lookahead and multi-pass statistics
text: Framewright's default 150-frame live lookahead window adds a median of 5.1 seconds of glass-to-glass latency attributable specifically to buffering, on top of encode and network delivery latency accounted for elsewhere in the pipeline. Reducing the window to 60 frames, a common configuration for latency-sensitive interactive streams, cuts buffering-attributable latency to roughly 2.0 seconds while reducing measured rate-control accuracy, tracked as deviation from target bitrate per segment, by approximately 15% relative to the default window. The standard two-pass VOD pipeline, by contrast, adds no comparable latency concern since it operates on a complete asset rather than a live stream, though first-pass analysis alone adds an average of 0.4x real-time processing overhead before the second, full-quality pass begins.

title: History: from fixed-ladder single-pass encoding to per-title optimization
text: Framewright began in 2016 as a single-pass constant-rate-factor encoder supporting only H.264, built to replace a fixed-quantization encoding pipeline that produced wildly inconsistent perceived quality across content of different complexity at the same nominal setting. That initial rate-factor approach improved quality consistency substantially but offered no path to the strict, predictable output bitrates live streaming and bandwidth-constrained delivery require. The first production release, in 2018, added a two-pass variable-bitrate mode specifically to serve that need, using a first analysis pass to inform second-pass bit allocation against a fixed target bitrate rather than a fixed quality target. HEVC support arrived in 2019, offering meaningfully better compression efficiency than H.264 for the same perceptual quality, though adoption among partner platforms was gated for years afterward by inconsistent decoder support across client devices. The most consequential architectural change came in 2021, when Framewright replaced its original PSNR-based distortion estimate with a perceptual-quality-driven model calibrated against human subjective rating panels, after internal testing repeatedly found that PSNR-optimized encodes sometimes looked visibly worse to human viewers than alternate encodes with lower PSNR scores but better-preserved perceptually salient detail. That perceptual-model transition required re-deriving the Lagrangian lambda-to-QP relationship essentially from scratch, since the old relationship had been tuned specifically against PSNR and did not transfer cleanly to the new distortion metric. AV1 support followed in 2023, and per-title, per-scene adaptive bitrate-ladder generation, replacing a single content-agnostic ladder applied uniformly across the library, shipped in 2025 after several years of internal experimentation with genre-specific and eventually fully per-asset ladder generation. Looking back, the engineering team identifies the 2021 shift to perceptual-quality-driven optimization as the single most consequential change, since per-title encoding, later codec support, and ongoing calibration work all build on a distortion model that PSNR-based optimization could never have supported in the first place, regardless of how much engineering effort had been invested in tuning it further. Engineers who worked through the PSNR-to-perceptual transition describe the hardest part as not the algorithmic change itself but convincing partner platforms, several of which had built quality-monitoring dashboards around PSNR, that a lower-PSNR encode could genuinely look better to viewers, a claim that took months of side-by-side subjective testing to demonstrate rather than being accepted on theory alone. That experience shaped how later changes were introduced: both the AV1 rollout and the per-title ladder generator shipped with extensive partner-facing subjective comparison material from the outset. The team also notes that HEVC's slow adoption, gated by client decoder support rather than anything within Framewright's control, taught an adjacent lesson: encoder-side improvements only translate into real-world benefit as quickly as the surrounding device ecosystem can absorb them.

title: Incident postmortem: the January 2024 sports content quality regression
text: On January 9th, 2024, Framewright shipped a routine update to its perceptual quality model, intended to improve calibration accuracy for animated content based on several months of accumulated subjective rating data, alongside the regularly scheduled release. Within 72 hours, partner platforms serving live and recently encoded sports content began reporting a measurable increase in viewer-reported quality complaints, concentrated specifically around fast-motion broadcast segments such as replays and rapid camera pans. Investigation traced the root cause to an unintended interaction between the animated-content calibration update and the model's shared motion-sensitivity component: the update had adjusted a shared parameter governing how strongly motion blur suppresses perceived distortion, tuned specifically to better match animated content's comparatively simple motion patterns, without adequately testing the parameter's effect on the complex, high-frequency motion typical of live sports broadcasts. The adjusted parameter caused the model to systematically underestimate perceptual distortion in high-motion sports scenes, which in turn caused the rate-distortion optimizer to under-allocate bits to exactly those scenes, producing visible quality degradation that the model's own scoring metric failed to flag because the same miscalibrated model was used both to guide encoding decisions and to verify their quality. Automated quality-compliance monitoring, which relies on the perceptual score to detect regressions, therefore showed segments passing their target score even as human viewers reported visibly degraded quality, since the scoring model and the allocation model shared the exact same blind spot. The regression went undetected by automated systems for the full 72 hours until accumulating viewer complaints prompted a manual investigation, at which point side-by-side comparison against the pre-update model's scores immediately revealed the discrepancy. The immediate remediation reverted the shared motion-sensitivity parameter to its pre-update value while preserving the unrelated animated-content calibration improvements that had prompted the release. A lasting fix, shipped six weeks later, split the previously shared motion-sensitivity parameter into genre-aware variants calibrated independently for animated and high-motion live-action content, closing the specific coupling that had caused the regression. The postmortem's authors recommended that any future perceptual-model update affecting a parameter shared across multiple content categories be validated against held-out subjective ratings from every affected category, not only the category the update was originally intended to improve, before shipping to production. A secondary recommendation addressed the blind-spot directly: quality-compliance monitoring now cross-checks a sample of segments against an independently frozen reference model, retrained far less often than the production scoring model, so a shared miscalibration cannot simultaneously corrupt both encoding and the verification meant to catch it. The public incident disclosure credited partners' own viewer-complaint pipelines, rather than any internal monitoring system, with surfacing the regression, a gap the postmortem treated as at least as significant as the calibration bug itself.

title: Current status and roadmap
text: Framewright's rate-control engine sits at version 5.6 as of the most recent release, with genre-aware motion-sensitivity calibration, shipped after the January 2024 regression, now applied by default across every supported content category. The engineering team's near-term roadmap prioritizes extending per-title, per-scene bitrate-ladder generation to live-streaming workloads, currently limited to the two-pass VOD pipeline due to its reliance on full-asset complexity statistics unavailable in a live context. A longer-term research effort is exploring whether a lightweight, forward-looking complexity estimate derived from the existing lookahead window could approximate enough of per-title encoding's benefit for live streams to justify the added encoding-pipeline complexity. None of these planned changes are expected to alter the core Lagrangian rate-distortion framework underlying every encoding mode, which the team considers well validated despite the occasional calibration-related incident.
